steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The sentence has two independent clauses: (1) "Sei Shōnagon composed many [blank]" and (2) "she bemoans boats run aground, toppled trees, and defeated wrestlers." Between them sits a long prepositional phrase introducing the specific list being discussed. The blank must create a grammatically legal boundary between these two clauses.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — "lists;": A semicolon requires an independent clause on both sides. What follows the semicolon here is "'Things That Have Lost Their Power,' which appears in her tenth-century account..." — this begins with a quoted title, not an independent clause in context. The result is a semicolon before a prepositional phrase, which is a boundary error. ✗
  • Choice B — "lists in": This creates no sentence boundary at all, fusing both clauses with a preposition into a run-on: "Sei Shōnagon composed many lists in 'Things That Have Lost Their Power'... she bemoans..." — no conjunction or punctuation separates the two main clauses. ✗
  • Choice C — "lists. In": The period ends the first independent clause cleanly: "Sei Shōnagon composed many lists." The word "In" then begins the second sentence with a prepositional phrase — "In 'Things That Have Lost Their Power,' which appears in her Pillow Book, she bemoans..." — which correctly leads into the subject "she" and verb "bemoans." ✓
  • Choice D — "lists:": A colon after an independent clause must introduce a list, explanation, or quotation that the first clause directly sets up. Here it would be followed by "'Things That Have Lost Their Power,' which appears..." — a prepositional phrase, not an explanation of "lists." The colon is grammatically misused. ✗
That matches choice C — C is correct.
